plainMeaning: This verse calls ten more great souls to mind — Metārya, Sthūlibhadra, Vajra, Nandiṣeṇa, Siṁhagiri, Kṛtapuṇya, Sukośala, Puṇḍarīka, Keśī and Karakaṇḍu — whose noble lives we remember so we may be inspired to live like them.
recitation: meajja thūlibhaddo, vayararisī nandiseṇa siṁhagiri | kayavanno a sukosala, puṇḍario kesi karakaṇḍu

wordByWord.gloss: Metārya — the ascetic who let a goldsmith bind and crush his head rather than betray the crane that had swallowed the gold grains
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit meajja / meyajja < Sanskrit metārya (also metejja); a brahmin lineage-name
wordByWord.notes: Here the Āvaśyaka monk-martyr Metārya, not — despite the shared Sanskrit name — Mahāvīra's tenth gaṇadhara Metārya.

wordByWord.gloss: Sthūlibhadra — the monk who held perfect celibacy through the rains in the courtesan Kośā's house
wordByWord.etymology: sthūla ('great, stout') + bhadra ('auspicious'); Prakrit -bhaddo for Sanskrit -bhadraḥ

wordByWord.gloss: Vajra-ṛṣi (Vajrasvāmī) — the boy-prodigy, last master of the ten Pūrvas
wordByWord.etymology: vajra ('diamond / thunderbolt') + risī < ṛṣi ('seer'); Prakrit vayara for Sanskrit vajra

wordByWord.gloss: Nandiṣeṇa — renowned for tireless vaiyāvṛttya (selfless service to ascetics)
wordByWord.etymology: nandi ('joy') + seṇa < sena ('host'); Prakrit -seṇa for Sanskrit -ṣeṇa
wordByWord.notes: The tradition knows more than one Nandiṣeṇa; the service-exemplar is the likeliest intent here, though recensions vary.

wordByWord.gloss: Siṁhagiri — the ācārya who was Vajrasvāmī's preceptor
wordByWord.notes: Book spelling સિંહગિરિ; also met as સીહગિરી in other recensions.

wordByWord.gloss: Kṛtapuṇya ('he who has made merit') — whose store of giving and merit bore its fruit
wordByWord.etymology: kaya < kṛta ('done, made') + vanna < puṇya ('merit'); Prakrit kayavanna for Sanskrit kṛtapuṇya
Prakrit a / ya < Sanskrit ca, the connective particle
wordByWord.notes: Links the litany; metrically light, semantically only 'and'.

wordByWord.gloss: Sukośala — the muni devoured in kāyotsarga by his own mother reborn as a tigress, unmoved to the last and so liberated
wordByWord.etymology: su ('good, noble') + kosala (the realm/name Kośala); Sanskrit sukośala

wordByWord.gloss: Puṇḍarīka — who by one day of true-hearted renunciation rose to heaven, paired against his lapsed brother Kaṇḍarīka
wordByWord.etymology: puṇḍarīka ('white lotus'); Prakrit puṇḍario for Sanskrit puṇḍarīkaḥ

wordByWord.gloss: Keśī (Keśī-kumāra-śramaṇa) — the learned ascetic of Pārśva's lineage who met Gautama in concord
wordByWord.etymology: keśin ('the long-haired one'); Prakrit kesi for Sanskrit keśī

wordByWord.gloss: Karakaṇḍu — the Pratyekabuddha-king of Kaliṅga who renounced on seeing his once-mighty bull grown decrepit
wordByWord.etymology: karakaṇḍu, a proper name; traditionally one of the four Pratyekabuddhas

literalTranslation: Metārya, Sthūlibhadra, Vajra-ṛṣi, Nandiṣeṇa, Siṁhagiri; Kṛtapuṇya and Sukośala, Puṇḍarīka, Keśī, Karakaṇḍu.
idiomaticTranslation: Remember Metārya and Sthūlibhadra, the sage Vajra, Nandiṣeṇa and Siṁhagiri; Kṛtapuṇya too, and Sukośala, Puṇḍarīka, Keśī and Karakaṇḍu — souls whose very names, taken to heart, draw their virtue into our own.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This second gāthā widens the roll of mahāsattvas with ten further names, and its art lies in the variety of heroism it gathers in a single breath. It opens with, and turns again upon, two martyrs of equanimity: Metārya, who let a goldsmith bind and crush his head in the noon heat rather than name the crane that had swallowed the gold grains — guarding a bird's life with his own — and Sukośala, devoured in kāyotsarga by his own mother reborn as a tigress, unmoved to the last. Around them stand other registers of greatness: Sthūlibhadra's unbroken celibacy in the courtesan's house; Vajra-ṛṣi's prodigious mastery of the Pūrvas and his guru Siṁhagiri's preceptorship; Nandiṣeṇa's selfless service; Kṛtapuṇya's merit ripened from giving; Puṇḍarīka, whose single day of sincere renunciation outweighed a lifetime of hollow asceticism, set against his fallen brother Kaṇḍarīka; Keśī, the Pārśva-line teacher whose humility met Gautama in agreement; and Karakaṇḍu, the self-awakened king roused to dispassion by one decrepit bull. The verse thus quietly teaches that the summit is reached by many doors — endurance, chastity, learning, service, generosity, inner resolve, right view, and dispassion — and that each, fully lived, is heroic. No deed is narrated; the names alone are set ringing, trusting the hearer to supply the remembered life behind each.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Several names here embody parīṣaha-jaya and upasarga-titikṣā — the conquest of affliction. Metārya and Sukośala show kāyotsarga not as posture but as the soul's refusal to be moved by the body's destruction, the very stillness in which kevala-jñāna or liberation dawns; Metārya's shielding of the crane makes ahiṁsā costlier than life itself. Puṇḍarīka encodes the primacy of bhāva over dravya — that inward sincerity, not the sheer length of ascetic externals, decides one's gati. Karakaṇḍu exemplifies pratyekabuddha-bodhi: awakening kindled by a single sight of impermanence (anityatā), without a teacher. Together they map the Jain conviction that karma is shed by enduring its fruit with equanimity rather than by reacting to it.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Taken as name-remembrance, the verse hands the reciter ready exemplars for the day's trials: when wronged or falsely accused, recall Metārya's compassion-under-torture; when the body suffers, Sukośala's steadiness; when tempted, Sthūlibhadra; when asked to serve, Nandiṣeṇa. One short uttered name can re-aim a wavering resolve toward the virtue it stands for.

crossReferences.text: Karakaṇḍu among the four Pratyekabuddhas, Uttarādhyayana-sūtra, Lecture 18 (Sañjaya)
Metārya's martyrdom, Āvaśyaka Niryukti 869–870 with cūrṇi

sources.title: Metarya (Metārya) — Wisdom Library
sources.consultedFor: Identifying Meajja as the monk-martyr Metārya (crane/jewels story), his sāmāyika and ṛṣi status, and the Āvaśyaka references; distinguishing him from the gaṇadhara Metārya

sources.title: Karakandu (Karakaṇḍu) — Wisdom Library
sources.consultedFor: Karakaṇḍu as a Pratyekabuddha, the aging-bull awakening, and his place in litany-homage works such as the Sādhuvandanā

sources.title: Keshi (Keśī) — Wisdom Library
sources.consultedFor: Disambiguating Keśī and confirming the Pārśva-lineage śramaṇa Keśī-kumāra

sources.title: Sthulabhadra — Wikipedia
sources.consultedFor: Sthūlibhadra's life and his celibacy exemplar in the courtesan Kośā's house

notes: મેઅજ્જ (Meajja) here denotes the ascetic martyr Metārya of the Āvaśyaka crane-story, not Mahāvīra's tenth gaṇadhara of the same Sanskrit name (Metārya); the two are distinct figures. Orthographic/recension variants are kept per the source book: સિંહગિરિ also appears as સીહગિરી, and કયવન્નો as કયવન્ના elsewhere. More than one Nandiṣeṇa is known to the tradition (a vaiyāvṛttya-exemplar and a monk detained by a courtesan who later returned); the service-exemplar is the likeliest intent but is not certain. Identifications follow the standard kathā tradition; where a life is transmitted in variant forms, only the broadly attested outline is given.
